description: Web Audio API-generated samples. Students identify interval or chord quality from four choices. Inspired by TonedEar.com — RockEar adds rock context, kid-friendly UX, and XP/streak engagement.

beginner.label: Unison
beginner.explanation: Same note played twice — zero distance between them.

beginner.label: Octave
beginner.explanation: Double the frequency — same note, one register higher.

beginner.label: Perfect 5th
beginner.explanation: The power chord interval. Foundation of rock harmony.

beginner.label: Perfect 4th
beginner.explanation: Inverts to a 5th. Sounds open and stable.

beginner.label: Major 2nd
beginner.explanation: A whole step. Distance between most adjacent scale notes.

beginner.label: Minor 2nd
beginner.explanation: The tightest interval — one half step. Very dissonant.

beginner.label: Major 3rd
beginner.explanation: The interval that makes a chord sound major and bright.

beginner.label: Minor 3rd
beginner.explanation: Lower the 3rd by a half step — chord goes dark.

beginner.label: Major 6th
beginner.explanation: Warm and bright. Common in vocal melodies and double-stops.

beginner.label: Unison vs Octave
beginner.audioNote: C4-C4 vs C4-C5 — which is wider?
beginner.explanation: Octave spans 12 semitones; unison is zero. Train your ears to hear the gap.

beginner.label: P4 vs P5 — identify
beginner.audioNote: C4 then either F4 or G4
beginner.explanation: P5 is wider — 7 semitones vs 5. P5 sounds more open and hollow.

beginner.label: Major 2nd ascending
beginner.explanation: Whole step up. The distance between scale degrees 1 and 2.

beginner.label: Minor 3rd — different root
beginner.explanation: Same interval, different starting note. Train the ear, not memory.

beginner.label: Perfect 5th — different root
beginner.explanation: G to D — same P5 sound regardless of root.

beginner.label: Major 3rd — different root
beginner.explanation: E to G# — bright and stable, like the top two strings of a major chord.

beginner.label: Octave — different root
beginner.explanation: Same pitch name, one register apart — always 12 semitones.

beginner.label: Perfect 4th — different root
beginner.explanation: G to C — same open, stable P4 sound.

beginner.label: Major 2nd — different root
beginner.explanation: F to G — one whole step, same as any adjacent scale notes.

beginner.label: Minor 3rd — low register
beginner.explanation: Lower register — same interval, muddier sound. Train across octaves.

beginner.label: P5 — low register
beginner.explanation: Classic open-string guitar tuning interval. Low but recognizable.

beginner.label: Random: M2 or M3?
beginner.audioNote: C4 then D4 OR C4 then E4
beginner.explanation: Two most common ascending intervals. Focus on how wide the leap feels.

beginner.label: Random: P4 or P5?
beginner.audioNote: Random root, either F4 or G4 above C4
beginner.explanation: Distinguish 5 vs 7 semitones. P5 sounds noticeably more open.

beginner.label: M3 vs m3 — side by side
beginner.explanation: Hear the brightness drop when that half step lowers.

beginner.label: Interval direction — ascending
beginner.explanation: Same pitches, direction matters. G is above C — ascending.

beginner.label: Interval direction — descending
beginner.explanation: G down to C — same interval, opposite direction, different feel.

intermediate.label: Tritone
intermediate.explanation: Splits the octave exactly in half. Maximum tension, wants to resolve.

intermediate.label: Minor 6th
intermediate.explanation: Darker than major 6th. One half step narrows it significantly.

intermediate.label: Minor 7th
intermediate.explanation: Dominant 7th chord interval — tension seeking resolution downward.

intermediate.label: Major 7th
intermediate.explanation: One half step below octave — dreamy and unresolved.

intermediate.label: Tritone — different root
intermediate.explanation: F# to C — same unstable quality regardless of starting note.

intermediate.label: Minor 7th — different root
intermediate.explanation: G to F — classic dominant 7th sound.

intermediate.label: Major 7th — different root
intermediate.explanation: D to C# — one half step from the octave, very tense.

intermediate.label: m6 vs M6 — side by side
intermediate.explanation: Hear the warmth drop when the 6th lowers by a half step.

intermediate.label: m7 vs M7 — side by side
intermediate.explanation: Minor 7th is wider but sounds more grounded; major 7th is tenser.

intermediate.label: Tritone vs P4 — side by side
intermediate.explanation: One half step turns stability into tension. Critical ear training distinction.

intermediate.label: Tritone vs P5 — side by side
intermediate.explanation: Tritone sounds unfinished; P5 resolves and settles.

intermediate.label: Chromatic: identify from full set
intermediate.explanation: No hints. Any interval from the chromatic set. Listen carefully.

intermediate.label: Descending m3
intermediate.explanation: Same interval, going down. Slightly different emotional quality.

intermediate.label: Descending P5
intermediate.explanation: G down to C — power chord inverted downward.

intermediate.label: Descending M6
intermediate.explanation: A down to C — wide, warm descent.

intermediate.label: Compound: Major 9th
intermediate.explanation: M2 + octave. Extended interval — spacious and open-sounding.

intermediate.label: Compound: Minor 9th
intermediate.explanation: m2 + octave. Very tense — dissonant even in a wide voicing.

intermediate.label: Low register tritone
intermediate.explanation: Low register tritone — ominous and unstable. The lowest sounding tritones have maximum weight.

intermediate.label: Interval in melody context
intermediate.explanation: Identify the final interval within a short melodic phrase.

intermediate.label: Interval in melody context
intermediate.explanation: Descending phrase — identify bottom interval.

intermediate.label: Speed round: M3 or m3?
intermediate.explanation: 3-second window. Major or minor 3rd — no other options.

intermediate.label: Speed round: P4 or Tritone?
intermediate.explanation: One half step difference — the most important distinction in Western music.

intermediate.label: Speed round: m7 or M7?
intermediate.explanation: One half step apart — focus on the tension level.

intermediate.label: Harmonic interval (simultaneous)
intermediate.explanation: Both notes at once — identify the harmonic interval.

intermediate.label: Harmonic interval (simultaneous)
intermediate.explanation: Harmonic tritone — maximum dissonance heard simultaneously.

advanced.label: Full set ID — ascending
advanced.explanation: No hints. Any interval, any root. Full chromatic set in play.

advanced.label: Full set ID — descending
advanced.explanation: Direction is descending. Adjust your ear — same interval sounds different going down.

advanced.label: Full set ID — harmonic
advanced.explanation: No melodic clue — pure harmonic identification.

advanced.label: Compound: Major 9th vs Minor 9th
advanced.explanation: Wide intervals — one half step difference across an octave.

advanced.label: Compound: Perfect 11th
advanced.explanation: P4 + octave. Common in open jazz and extended rock voicings.

advanced.label: Compound: Perfect 12th
advanced.explanation: P5 + octave. Natural harmonic relationship — very open and pure.

advanced.label: Interval in 4-note melody
advanced.audioNote: Ascending melody — identify the largest leap
advanced.explanation: Four notes — find the widest interval in the phrase.

advanced.label: Interval in chord voicing
advanced.audioNote: Identify interval between bass and soprano
advanced.explanation: Real musical context — outer voices of a 4-part chord.

advanced.label: Enharmonic interval
advanced.audioNote: C4 then A#4 (same pitch as Bb4)
advanced.explanation: A# and Bb sound identical — interval name depends on context.

advanced.label: Augmented 4th vs Diminished 5th
advanced.audioNote: Both are tritones — identify by context
advanced.explanation: Both enharmonic tritones — the ear hears one thing, theory names two.

advanced.label: Speed round: any interval, 2 sec
advanced.explanation: 2-second window. No time to analyze — trust your ear.

advanced.label: Interval sequence: identify both
advanced.audioNote: Two intervals in sequence, name each
advanced.explanation: Multi-step recall. Keep the first interval in memory while hearing the second.

advanced.label: Descending tritone
advanced.explanation: Same unstable quality in either direction.

advanced.label: Descending M7
advanced.explanation: One half step above the unison going down — very tight and tense.

advanced.label: Harmonic m9 — very dissonant
advanced.explanation: Close-voiced minor 9th — maximum harmonic tension.

advanced.label: Guitar string intervals
advanced.explanation: Standard guitar tuning is P4 between every adjacent string (except G-B).

advanced.label: Guitar string intervals — exception
advanced.explanation: The G-B exception — M3, not P4. Critical for guitarists to internalize.

advanced.label: Interval inversion: P5 becomes?
advanced.audioNote: Invert a P5 — what do you hear?
advanced.explanation: Inversion rule: P5 inverts to P4 (9 - 5 = 4). Same notes, flipped.

advanced.label: Interval inversion: M3 becomes?
advanced.audioNote: Invert a M3 — what do you hear?
advanced.explanation: M3 inverts to m6. Major inverts to minor; 3 + 6 = 9.

advanced.label: Interval in rock riff context
advanced.audioNote: Riff uses repeated P5 then drops m3
advanced.explanation: Identify the final interval drop in a short rock riff phrase.

advanced.label: Microtonal awareness — in tune vs sharp
advanced.explanation: Advanced pitch discrimination — is the interval pure or tempered?

advanced.label: Chord tone: identify interval above root
advanced.audioNote: Major chord — identify M3 above root
advanced.explanation: Isolate one interval within a full chord — the defining tone.

advanced.label: Chord tone: identify P5 above root
advanced.audioNote: Minor chord — identify P5 above root
advanced.explanation: The 5th is present in both major and minor chords — same interval.

advanced.label: Random full set — harmonic, fast
advanced.explanation: Full harmonic set, speed mode. Elite ear training.

advanced.label: Interval ear test: melodic context
advanced.audioNote: Short melodic phrase — identify the opening interval.
advanced.explanation: Apply interval knowledge to a melodic phrase. The ultimate test of ear training in context.

beginner.label: Major Chord
beginner.explanation: Bright and resolved. The most common chord in rock and pop.

beginner.label: Minor Chord
beginner.explanation: Darker and more emotional. Lowered 3rd is the defining factor.

beginner.label: Power Chord
beginner.explanation: Root and 5th only — no 3rd. Sounds massive through distortion.

beginner.label: Major — different root
beginner.explanation: Same bright quality regardless of root. Train the color, not the note.

beginner.label: Minor — different root
beginner.explanation: Em — the most common minor chord in rock guitar.

beginner.label: Major vs Minor — side by side
beginner.explanation: Hear the 3rd shift from E to Eb — one half step, totally different feel.

beginner.label: Power chord vs Major
beginner.explanation: Power chord is hollow — no 3rd means no major or minor quality.

beginner.label: Major — high register
beginner.audioNote: E major voiced high on guitar
beginner.explanation: Same chord quality in a brighter register.

beginner.label: Minor — low register
beginner.explanation: Low voicing — darker timbre, same minor quality.

beginner.label: Major with distortion
beginner.explanation: Distortion muddies 3rds — train your ear to hear quality through gain.

beginner.label: Minor with distortion
beginner.explanation: Minor quality survives distortion — the 3rd still colors the sound.

beginner.label: Identify root + quality
beginner.explanation: Two-part ID: root note and chord quality together.

beginner.label: Identify root + quality
beginner.explanation: Root ID plus quality — building toward full chord recognition.

beginner.label: Major chord — arpeggiated
beginner.audioNote: C major played one note at a time
beginner.explanation: Notes spread out — same chord, melodic delivery.

beginner.label: Minor chord — arpeggiated
beginner.audioNote: A minor played one note at a time
beginner.explanation: Arpeggiated minor — common in fingerpicked rock intros.

beginner.label: Speed: Major or Minor?
beginner.explanation: Quick fire — just major or minor, no other options.

beginner.label: Major from 3 options
beginner.audioNote: C major, G major, D major — which plays?
beginner.explanation: Same quality, different roots — identify the root by ear.

beginner.label: Strummed vs picked — same chord
beginner.audioNote: E major strummed vs E major picked
beginner.explanation: Articulation changes, chord quality doesn't.

beginner.label: Major in open position
beginner.explanation: Open string voicings ring differently — same quality.

beginner.label: Minor in open position
beginner.explanation: Em open — two-finger chord, full minor quality.

beginner.label: Chord in piano timbre
beginner.explanation: Timbre changes — piano vs guitar. Quality stays the same.

beginner.label: Chord in piano timbre
beginner.explanation: Piano minor — rounder attack, same darker quality.

beginner.label: Major 3-chord recognition
beginner.audioNote: I-IV-V in C major — all major?
beginner.explanation: Basic I-IV-V — all major chords, the foundation of rock progressions.

beginner.label: Mixed quality in progression
beginner.explanation: Identify chord quality within a moving progression.

beginner.label: Final chord quality
beginner.audioNote: Progression ends — is it major or minor?
beginner.explanation: Resolution detection — does the song feel finished and bright?

intermediate.label: Dominant 7th
intermediate.explanation: Tension wants to resolve to the tonic. Cornerstone of blues.

intermediate.label: Minor 7th
intermediate.explanation: Smoother than straight minor. Common in funk, soul, and indie rock.

intermediate.label: Major 7th
intermediate.explanation: Lush and dreamy. The B above the G is the defining tone.

intermediate.label: Suspended 4th
intermediate.explanation: No 3rd — replaced by P4. Unresolved. Common in rock intros.

intermediate.label: Suspended 2nd
intermediate.explanation: No 3rd — replaced by M2. Open and airy. Common in alternative rock.

intermediate.label: Dom7 vs Maj7 — side by side
intermediate.explanation: Dom7 has a minor 7th interval (F); Maj7 has a major 7th (F#). Hear the difference.

intermediate.label: Min7 vs Dom7 — side by side
intermediate.explanation: Both have a minor 7th — but dom7 has a major 3rd; min7 has a minor 3rd.

intermediate.label: Sus4 vs Major — side by side
intermediate.explanation: Sus resolves to major when the 4th drops to the 3rd.

intermediate.label: Add9 chord
intermediate.explanation: Major chord plus a 9th (D) — fuller than straight major, no 7th.

intermediate.label: Minor 7th — different root
intermediate.explanation: Same warm, extended minor quality — different root.

intermediate.label: Dominant 7th — different root
intermediate.explanation: E7 — classic blues/rock dominant. Hear that minor 7th pull.

intermediate.label: Major 7th — different root
intermediate.explanation: Fmaj7 — lush and spacious. Common in indie and ambient rock.

intermediate.label: Chord in progression: identify 7th type
intermediate.audioNote: IV chord in blues — is it dom7 or maj7?
intermediate.explanation: Blues uses dominant 7th on I, IV, and V — not major 7th.

intermediate.label: Sus2 vs Add9
intermediate.audioNote: Dsus2 vs Dadd9 — both have a 2nd/9th
intermediate.explanation: Sus2 replaces the 3rd; Add9 keeps it. Hear the extra fullness in add9.

intermediate.label: Chord quality in distorted context
intermediate.explanation: Extended chords through distortion — the 7th colors the sound even with gain.

intermediate.label: Minor 7th arpeggiated
intermediate.explanation: Spread voicing — same color, melodic delivery.

intermediate.label: Speed: 7th chord type?
intermediate.explanation: Three-way 7th chord ID. 3-second window.

intermediate.label: Dominant 7th resolving to I
intermediate.audioNote: G7 to C major — identify the resolution
intermediate.explanation: Hear V7 resolving to I — the most common cadence in tonal music.

intermediate.label: Chord quality: piano timbre
intermediate.explanation: Piano timbre — same lush maj7 quality.

intermediate.label: Major vs Sus — in context
intermediate.explanation: Sus4 resolving to major within a progression — hear the release.

intermediate.label: Full quality ID from 5 options
intermediate.audioNote: Random chord from full intermediate set
intermediate.explanation: Major, minor, dom7, min7, maj7, sus4, sus2 all in play.

intermediate.label: Identify chord in bass register
intermediate.explanation: Low voicing adds mud — still identify the quality.

intermediate.label: Add9 vs maj9
intermediate.explanation: Maj9 = major 7th + 9th. Add9 = just the 9th added, no 7th.

intermediate.label: Chord in song context
intermediate.audioNote: Verse uses one chord type throughout — identify
intermediate.explanation: Extended listening — maintain identification over several bars.

intermediate.label: Chord change detection
intermediate.explanation: Rhythmic precision meets harmonic ID — locate the change.

advanced.label: Diminished Triad
advanced.explanation: Two stacked minor 3rds. Maximum tension. Common in metal.

advanced.label: Augmented Triad
advanced.explanation: Two stacked major 3rds. Unstable and dreamy — common in prog rock.

advanced.label: Half-Diminished (m7b5)
advanced.explanation: Diminished triad with a minor 7th. The ii chord in minor keys.

advanced.label: Diminished 7th
advanced.explanation: All minor 3rds stacked — symmetrical, maximum tension.

advanced.label: Minor Major 7th
advanced.explanation: Minor chord with a major 7th — exotic, tense, cinematic.

advanced.label: Major 9th
advanced.explanation: Full major 7th + 9th. Lush and extended. Jazz and ambient rock.

advanced.label: Minor 9th
advanced.explanation: Minor 7th + 9th. Rich and moody. Neo-soul and jazz fusion.

advanced.label: Dominant 9th
advanced.explanation: Dom7 + 9th. Funky and full. Classic R&B and blues-rock.

advanced.label: Aug vs Dim — side by side
advanced.explanation: Aug = bright tension (raised 5th); Dim = dark tension (lowered 5th).

advanced.label: Half-dim vs Dim7 — side by side
advanced.explanation: Half-dim has a minor 7th; Dim7 has a diminished 7th (one lower).

advanced.label: Chord with added color tone: dom7#9
advanced.audioNote: C major with added b7 and #9
advanced.explanation: The dominant 7#9 chord — a major 3rd and minor 3rd coexist, creating a tense, bluesy color.

advanced.label: Identify chord in jazz context — ii
advanced.audioNote: ii-V-I in C — identify the ii chord
advanced.explanation: The ii chord in jazz ii-V-I is always minor 7th.

advanced.label: Identify chord in jazz context — V
advanced.audioNote: ii-V-I in C — identify the V chord
advanced.explanation: The V chord is always dominant 7th — that's what creates the resolution pull.

advanced.label: Chord inversion: first inversion
advanced.explanation: The 3rd in the bass changes the color and weight of the chord.

advanced.label: Chord inversion: second inversion
advanced.explanation: The 5th in the bass — less stable, often used as a passing chord.

advanced.label: Chord quality in modal context
advanced.audioNote: Dorian mode — what quality is the IV chord?
advanced.explanation: In Dorian, the IV chord is major (raised 6th) — distinguishes it from natural minor.

advanced.label: Full advanced set speed round
advanced.audioNote: Random from full advanced chord set
advanced.explanation: All chord types in play. 3-second window.

advanced.label: Chord in dense mix
advanced.audioNote: Identify chord quality in a full band context
advanced.explanation: Real-world skill — guitar, bass, drums, vocals all playing.

advanced.label: Borrowed chord identification
advanced.audioNote: bVII in major key — what quality?
advanced.explanation: bVII borrowed from parallel minor is always a major chord.

advanced.label: Secondary dominant
advanced.audioNote: V/V in C major — what chord?
advanced.explanation: D7 is the V of V in C major — a secondary dominant creating extra pull.

advanced.label: Polychord identification
advanced.explanation: Two separate harmonies layered — the slash chord sound.

advanced.label: Quartal voicing
advanced.explanation: Stacked 4ths — no clear major/minor quality. Modal and open.

advanced.label: Cluster chord
advanced.explanation: Adjacent semitones voiced together — atonal, harsh, used in experimental rock.

advanced.label: Chord progression: identify all qualities
advanced.explanation: Advanced progression analysis — sustained attention over multiple chords.

advanced.label: Chord in musical context
advanced.audioNote: Chord progression — identify the quality of the chord on beat 3.
advanced.explanation: Apply chord knowledge within a progression in real time.
